FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 4 (2020-2022), data release 6.0, N=4,443 adolescents recruited from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal cohort study using adjusted linear and Poisson regression models examining Year 2 exposures predicting Year 4 cardiovascular outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in BMC Public Health (2026); no indication of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found. The sole exposure is 'total recreational screen time' (Screen Time Questionnaire), which per methods (p.3) bundles 'watching television and videos, playing video games, texting, social media, and video chat' into a single composite hours/day score. Tables 2 and 3 report only this composite; no per-modality breakout (e.g., separate social media or texting coefficient) appears anywhere in main tables, supplementary tables S2-S6, or sensitivity analyses. Step 2: The composite includes TV/video-viewing and video games — non-phone/non-SM ingredients without a separate breakout — so it fails Step 2. NOT_MET.

notes: Composite 'recreational screen time' includes TV, video games, texting, social media, video chat — no per-modality breakout in any analysis. Fails FT-IC2 Step 1 (no phone/SM-specific estimate) and Step 2 (composite contains non-phone/non-SM items).

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Baseline N=11,878, data release 5.1, four waves (Years 1-4). Multisite U.S. cohort aged 9-10 at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multi-level longitudinal models (MLM) and latent growth curve analysis examining smartphone ownership age and informant discrepancy of internalizing problems across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Development and Psychopathology (2025); no indication of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Smartphone ownership (age of first smartphone, categorized as 8&under, 9&10, 11&12, 13&14, No Phone) is the qualifying SM/phone variable. Table 3 reports multi-level model estimates pairing smartphone ownership group with internalizing problems (the individual-trait outcome): e.g., Informant effect (beta_2) in 8&under group b = -0.85, SE = .08, p < .001; 9&10 group b = -0.68, SE = .04, p < .001. These are numeric estimates (b, beta, SE, p) where smartphone ownership is IV and youth internalizing problems (anxious/depressed, withdrawn/depressed) is the individual-trait-level outcome. FT-IC2 MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: MLM coefficients (b, beta, SE, p-values) in Table 3 and Table 4, latent growth curve slopes, and chi-square difference tests across smartphone ownership age groups.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis pairing smartphone ownership (qualifying phone variable) with youth internalizing problems (individual-trait outcome) with reported numeric results. Criterion MET jointly with IC3.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone ownership serves as IV (predictor/grouping variable) throughout; internalizing problems is the DV. FT-EC7 requires SM/phone to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere — here SM/phone is the IV. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Smartphone ownership age as IV predicting parent-child informant discrepancy of internalizing problems as outcome. Longitudinal design across 4 waves. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, T1 N=11,787, ABCD cohort aged 9-10 at baseline, recruited across 21 U.S. sites, longitudinal data across 4 waves.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: random-intercept crosslagged panel models (RI-CLPM) examining bidirectional associations between electronic media use and family conflict across 4 annual waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Family Psychology (2025); no indication of prior duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social EMU composite (defined in methods as 'social media + texting') has its own separate crosslagged path estimates reported for boys (Figure 3) and girls (Figure 4), with numeric path coefficients (e.g., girls: L_EB->L_E1 = -0.42***, L_E1->L_F2 = 0.27***) and text-reported values (p.1032: t1-t2 b = -0.59, beta = -.18, SE = .02, p < .001 for girls; t2-t3 b = 0.13, beta = .08, SE = .03, p < .001). Social EMU is exclusively social media + texting — phone/SM-specific items — paired with family conflict (an individual-trait/family-functioning outcome). FT-IC2 MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: crosslagged path coefficients (b, beta, SE, p-values) for total EMU, social EMU, video-viewing EMU, and video game EMU across Figures 1-5 and supporting text.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential RI-CLPM analysis pairing social EMU (qualifying phone/SM variable) with family conflict (individual-trait family-functioning outcome) with reported numeric results. Criterion MET jointly with IC3.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional RI-CLPM design: social EMU (and total EMU) appears as both IV (predicting later family conflict) and DV (family conflict predicting later EMU). SM/phone is IV in analyses, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Social EMU (social media + texting) as qualifying variable in bidirectional crosslagged model with family conflict as individual-trait outcome. Social EMU is exclusively phone/SM items. INCLUDE.
